note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence pauses. Never rewrite, summarize, or reorder. Block windows are MEASURED onsets (global FFT normalized cross-correlation of each block mp3 against the joined master; every block matched at NCC=1.0000, monotonic, ~0.36s inter-block tail). The nominal Sigma(audio+0.4) formula using ffprobe per-file durations overshoots the probed master by 0.54s (> 0.3 tolerance) because ffprobe's mp3 durations overstate the decoded sample counts; the measured onsets are the ground truth. Master ffprobe duration 236.434s.

phrases.text: Open the Team module, then under Sales Coaching, click QA Dashboard.
This area records how staff sell at the register and scores every interaction against a script.
Three pages hold it: the dashboard, the scripts behind it, and the conversations themselves.
Start with the four numbers up top.
Average score this month, how many conversations fed it,
the share clearing the seventy-five percent target, and the swing against last month.
Green and up means the team is trending the right way.
Look at the trend.
Twelve weeks of average score, drawn against a dashed line at the target.
When the solid line rides above the dash, the team is clearing the bar.
Right now it's climbing.
Now the distribution.
Every scored interaction falls into a letter grade, A through F.
Half of them land in the B band this month.
The A's and B's together are your at-or-above-target share, in one glance.
Here's the panel that earns its keep.
Seven behaviors the platform listens for on every call, from greeting to closing,
each with a hit rate.
Membership sits at forty-seven percent, in red, well below the rest.
There's the gap.
The system already flagged it.
The Coaching Opportunity callout takes that weakest number
and turns it into a sentence a manager can act on: focus training on the membership pitch.
Read this panel first when you open the dashboard.
On the right, the leaderboard.
Top performers this month by average score, each with their conversation count next to it.
Notice volume and score don't always line up.
The button underneath jumps to every conversation, but leave it for now.
Scroll to the recent activity table.
The last ten interactions across every location: who, when, where, how long, and the grade.
The script column shows only a dash, so it won't tell you which script scored the call.
For that, we open the scripts page.
Click Scoring Scripts.
Its own header: two active scripts, one draft,
seven checkpoints on average, and two hundred eighty-four scored this month.
That last number is the same pool the dashboard counted, just labelled differently.
This is the rulebook.
Each script defines what the AI grades and how it's weighted.
A default standard-wash script, a membership-focused one with a higher target,
and a win-back script still in draft.
You read these; a manager builds and edits them.
One note card sits below.
It's best-practice guidance on weighting: revenue behaviors like membership highest,
soft skills like greeting lowest, and a pass mark around seventy-five to eighty percent.
Helpful context, but it's just a note, not a setting.
Last page. Click Conversations.
Same headline numbers as the dashboard, plus one that matters most for triage: needs coaching.
Twelve interactions this month scored low enough to review.
That's your work queue.
This is the full log, all two hundred eighty-four records.
Search by employee or transcript, filter, or export, though we won't export today.
Each row is one scored interaction.
Ignore the checkpoints-met column here;
it reads zero of eight on every row, which is just demo data.
The real hit rates live on the dashboard.
Put the three together.
The dashboard flagged membership as weak.
The scripts page holds the script built to fix it.
And this log is where a manager pulls the actual calls to coach from.
One weak number, three pages, a real next step.
So, three pages, three questions.
How's the team and what's weak: the QA Dashboard.
What are we grading against: Scoring Scripts.
Show me the actual calls: Conversations.
You read all three; the managers you support act on them.
